query: cookie vs sessionStorage vs localStorage browser interview question

competitors.label: MDN Storage API
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for the Web Storage API, including localStorage and sessionStorage behavior, limits, and lifecycle semantics.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on Storage API semantics, browser behavior, and tab-session scope.
theirGaps: It is less direct on the full interview comparison against cookies and on the security trade-off between client-only storage and server round-trips.

realWorldUseCases: Cookies, sessionStorage, and localStorage all store data in the browser, but they differ in size limits, expiration, and how they are sent to the server. Security and expiration differ, so choose carefully; test storage limits and cross-tab behavior.
actionableExamples: Cookies - Can be read by both the browser and the server. - Often used for authentication tokens or session IDs. - Sent automatically with requests to matching domains.
followUpConfusion: Security Notes - Never store sensitive data (like passwords or tokens) in localStorage or sessionStorage — they are easily accessible by JavaScript. - For secure cookies, use the HttpOnly and Secure flags so they can’t be accessed via JavaScript and are only sent over HTTPS.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains storage behavior accurately, but our page is more explicit about the actual frontend choice between cookie round-trips and client-only persistence.
actionableExamples: The docs still have strong API examples for localStorage and sessionStorage, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more complete on the likely follow-ups: security flags for cookies, token-storage risk, and tab scope versus persistent browser storage.

actionableExamples: Add one compact cross-tab versus same-tab example if we later need more example separation over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical Storage API reference, but our page is stronger for this interview question because it compares cookies, sessionStorage, and localStorage as architecture choices rather than isolated APIs.

followUpConfusion: Security Notes - Never store sensitive data (like passwords or tokens) in localStorage or sessionStorage — they are easily accessible by JavaScript. - For secure cookies, use the HttpOnly and Secure flags so they can’t be accessed via JavaScript and are only sent over HTTPS.

competitors.label: javascript.info LocalStorage, sessionStorage
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is a topic-direct tutorial for localStorage and sessionStorage and is a strong practical comparator for browser storage behavior.
theirStrengths: It is practical on storage lifecycle, events, and browser persistence behavior.
theirGaps: It is less focused on comparing those APIs with cookies in one compact interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: Cookies, sessionStorage, and localStorage all store data in the browser, but they differ in size limits, expiration, and how they are sent to the server. Security and expiration differ, so choose carefully; test storage limits and cross-tab behavior.
actionableExamples: localStorage - Stores data **persistently** — it remains even after closing the browser. - Only accessible through JavaScript (never sent to the server).
followUpConfusion: Security Notes - Never store sensitive data (like passwords or tokens) in localStorage or sessionStorage — they are easily accessible by JavaScript. - For secure cookies, use the HttpOnly and Secure flags so they can’t be accessed via JavaScript and are only sent over HTTPS.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is strong on storage APIs themselves, but our page is more explicit about the cookie-versus-storage architecture choice teams actually face.
actionableExamples: Its storage examples are strong and topic-direct, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers the likely follow-up set more completely by adding server visibility, secure cookie flags, and tab isolation.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter cross-tab persistence example later if we need to create more example distance over javascript.info.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info is strong on Web Storage behavior, but our page is better for this prompt because it integrates cookies into the comparison and makes the security boundary explicit.
